Green's Functions in Condensed Matter: From Quantum Mechanics to Quantum Transport ​

📅 Date: 2026-03-31 | 🏷️ Tags: Condensed Matter, Quantum Transport, Many-Body Physics | 📂 Category: Condensed Matter Notes

Introduction ​

This note develops Green's functions from elementary quantum mechanics, assuming familiarity with the Schrödinger equation, eigenstates, and Dirac notation, but no prior knowledge of statistical mechanics or condensed matter physics. Every concept is derived explicitly.

Part I: The Single-Particle Propagator ​

Motivation: Why Propagators? ​

In single-particle quantum mechanics, the time evolution of a state is governed by the Schrödinger equation:

iℏ∂∂t|ψ(t)⟩=H^|ψ(t)⟩

For a time-independent Hamiltonian, the formal solution is:

|ψ(t)⟩=e−iH^(t−t0)/ℏ|ψ(t0)⟩=U^(t,t0)|ψ(t0)⟩

where U^(t,t0)=e−iH^(t−t0)/ℏ is the time evolution operator.

The propagator (or Green's function) is the position-space matrix element of this operator:

G(r,t;r′,t′)≡⟨r|U^(t,t′)|r′⟩=⟨r|e−iH^(t−t′)/ℏ|r′⟩

Physical meaning: G(r,t;r′,t′) is the probability amplitude for a particle starting at position r′ at time t′ to be found at position r at time t.

Evolution of Wavefunctions ​

Given an initial wavefunction ψ(r′,t′)=⟨r′|ψ(t′)⟩, the wavefunction at later time is:

ψ(r,t)=∫d3r′G(r,t;r′,t′)ψ(r′,t′)

Proof: Insert complete set ∫d3r′|r′⟩⟨r′|=1^:

ψ(r,t)=⟨r|ψ(t)⟩=⟨r|U^(t,t′)|ψ(t′)⟩=∫d3r′⟨r|U^(t,t′)|r′⟩⟨r′|ψ(t′)⟩

The Free Particle Propagator ​

For a free particle: H^0=p^22m=−ℏ22m∇2

The eigenstates are plane waves |k⟩ with ⟨r|k⟩=1Veik⋅r and eigenvalues Ek=ℏ2k22m.

Insert two complete sets ∑k|k⟩⟨k|=1^:

G0(r,t;r′,t′)=∑k,k′⟨r|k⟩⟨k|e−iH^0(t−t′)/ℏ|k′⟩⟨k′|r′⟩

Since H^0|k′⟩=Ek′|k′⟩:

⟨k|e−iH^0(t−t′)/ℏ|k′⟩=e−iEk′(t−t′)/ℏδkk′

Thus:

G0(r,t;r′,t′)=1V∑keik⋅(r−r′)e−iEk(t−t′)/ℏ

Taking the continuum limit 1V∑k→∫d3k(2π)3:

G0(r,t;r′,t′)=∫d3k(2π)3exp⁡[ik⋅(r−r′)−iℏk22m(t−t′)]

This is a Gaussian integral. Completing the square in the exponent:

Let q=k−m(r−r′)ℏ(t−t′), then:

G0(r,t;r′,t′)=(m2πiℏ(t−t′))3/2exp⁡(im(r−r′)22ℏ(t−t′))

This is the free particle propagator. Note the phase factor and the spreading with time ∼(t−t′)−3/2.

The Differential Equation for G ​

From the definition G(r,t;r′,t′)=⟨r|e−iH^(t−t′)/ℏ|r′⟩, differentiate with respect to t:

iℏ∂∂tG=⟨r|H^e−iH^(t−t′)/ℏ|r′⟩=⟨r|H^|r′⟩matrix element

For H^=−ℏ22m∇2+V(r):

⟨r|H^|r′⟩=(−ℏ22m∇2+V(r))δ(r−r′)

Therefore:

(iℏ∂∂t+ℏ22m∇2−V(r))G(r,t;r′,t′)=ℏδ(r−r′)δ(t−t′)

This shows G is the Green's function for the Schrödinger operator, with a delta-function source.

Part II: From Quantum Mechanics to Statistical Mechanics ​

The Problem: N∼1023 Particles ​

Condensed matter systems contain macroscopic numbers of particles. Tracking each particle's wavefunction is impossible. We need a statistical description.

Key insight: Even for a single particle at finite temperature, we don't know the exact quantum state—the system is in a mixed state described by probabilities.

The Density Matrix: Definition ​

A pure state is described by a state vector |ψ⟩. Expectation values are ⟨A^⟩=⟨ψ|A^|ψ⟩.

A mixed state is a statistical ensemble: the system is in state |ψi⟩ with probability pi, where ∑ipi=1.

The expectation value of an observable is the weighted average:

⟨A^⟩=∑ipi⟨ψi|A^|ψi⟩

Define the density operator (density matrix):

ρ^≡∑ipi|ψi⟩⟨ψi|

Then:

⟨A^⟩=∑ipi⟨ψi|A^|ψi⟩=∑ipiTr(|ψi⟩⟨ψi|A^)=Tr(ρ^A^)

Proof of the cyclic property used above:

For any states |α⟩, |β⟩ and operator A^:

⟨α|A^|β⟩=∑n⟨α|A^|n⟩⟨n|β⟩=∑n⟨n|β⟩⟨α|A^|n⟩=Tr(|β⟩⟨α|A^)

Properties of the Density Matrix ​

  1. Hermiticity: ρ^†=ρ^ (since pi are real)

  2. Normalization: Tr(ρ^)=1 (probabilities sum to 1)

  3. Positivity: ⟨ϕ|ρ^|ϕ⟩≥0 for all |ϕ⟩

  4. Pure vs. Mixed:

    • Pure state: ρ^=|ψ⟩⟨ψ|, so ρ^2=ρ^ and Tr(ρ^2)=1
    • Mixed state: Tr(ρ^2)<1

Thermal Equilibrium: The Canonical Ensemble ​

For a system at temperature T in contact with a heat bath, the probability of being in eigenstate |n⟩ with energy En follows the Boltzmann distribution:

pn=e−βEnZ,β=1kBT

where Z is the partition function ensuring normalization:

Z=∑ne−βEn=Tr(e−βH^)

The canonical density matrix is:

ρ^=e−βH^Z=e−βH^Tr(e−βH^)

Time Evolution of the Density Matrix ​

From the Schrödinger equation iℏddt|ψi(t)⟩=H^|ψi(t)⟩:

iℏddtρ^(t)=∑ipi(iℏddt|ψi⟩)⟨ψi|+∑ipi|ψi⟩(iℏddt⟨ψi|)=∑ipiH^|ψi⟩⟨ψi|−∑ipi|ψi⟩⟨ψi|H^=[H^,ρ^]

This is the von Neumann equation:

iℏddtρ^(t)=[H^,ρ^(t)]

Note the sign difference from the Heisenberg equation for operators.

Part III: Imaginary Time and Matsubara Green's Functions ​

The Problem with Real Time at T>0 ​

For T>0, we need thermal averages like ⟨A^(t)B^(0)⟩=Tr(ρ^A^(t)B^(0)).

The operator e−iH^t/ℏ oscillates forever. Thermal weights e−βEn decay exponentially. These two exponentials behave very differently.

Key insight: Define τ=it/ℏ (imaginary time, with units of inverse energy). Then:

e−iH^t/ℏ=e−H^τ

Both the thermal factor e−βH^ and the "evolution" factor e−H^τ are now decaying exponentials—same mathematical structure!

The Matsubara Green's Function: Definition ​

For fermions (we'll specialize to electrons in condensed matter), define the imaginary-time Green's function:

G(r,τ;r′,τ′)≡−⟨Tτψ^(r,τ)ψ^†(r′,τ′)⟩

where:

  • τ,τ′∈[0,βℏ] are imaginary times
  • Tτ is the imaginary-time ordering operator (larger τ to the left)
  • Field operators in imaginary-time Heisenberg picture:
ψ^(r,τ)=eH^τψ^(r)e−H^τψ^†(r′,τ′)=eH^τ′ψ^†(r′)e−H^τ′

Note: ψ^†(r,τ)≠[ψ^(r,τ)]† in general! The † on the field operator is part of the notation, not Hermitian conjugation of the operator at imaginary time.

Explicit Form ​

For τ>τ′:

G(τ,τ′)=−Tr(ρ^eH^τψ^(r)e−H^τeH^τ′ψ^†(r′)e−H^τ′)

For τ<τ′:

G(τ,τ′)=+Tr(ρ^eH^τ′ψ^†(r′)e−H^τ′eH^τψ^(r)e−H^τ)

The Antiperiodicity Property (KMS Condition) ​

Theorem: For fermions, G(τ+βℏ)=−G(τ) for −βℏ<τ<0.

Proof:

Consider τ∈[0,βℏ] and examine G(τ−βℏ) where τ−βℏ<0:

G(τ−βℏ)=+Tr(ρ^ψ^†(τ)ψ^(τ−βℏ))

since τ−βℏ<0<τ, the operator with larger time (τ) goes left.

Now use cyclic property of trace and ρ^=e−βH^/Z:

Tr(ρ^A^B^)=Tr(e−βH^A^B^)/Z=Tr(B^e−βH^A^)/Z

Insert eβH^e−βH^=1^:

=Tr(e−βH^eβH^B^e−βH^A^)/Z=Tr(ρ^eβH^B^e−βH^A^)

For B^=ψ^†(τ)=eH^τψ^†e−H^τ:

eβH^ψ^†(τ)e−βH^=eβH^eH^τψ^†e−H^τe−βH^=eH^(τ−βℏ)ψ^†e−H^(τ−βℏ)=ψ^†(τ−βℏ)

Therefore:

G(τ−βℏ)=+Tr(ρ^ψ^†(τ−βℏ)ψ^(τ−βℏ))=+⟨ψ^†(τ−βℏ)ψ^(τ−βℏ)⟩

Wait, let's be more careful. Let τ′=τ−βℏ<0:

Actually, let me restart more carefully. Define τ∈[0,βℏ]. We want to relate G(τ) to G(τ−βℏ).

For τ>0, G(τ)=−⟨ψ^(τ)ψ^†(0)⟩.

For the argument τ−βℏ<0, since this is negative, we have τ−βℏ<0, so:

G(τ−βℏ)=+⟨ψ^†(0)ψ^(τ−βℏ)⟩

Now using the cyclic property with ρ^=e−βH^/Z:

⟨ψ^†(0)ψ^(τ−βℏ)⟩=Tr(e−βH^ψ^†(0)ψ^(τ−βℏ))/Z=Tr(ψ^(τ−βℏ)e−βH^ψ^†(0))/Z=Tr(e−βH^eβH^ψ^(τ−βℏ)e−βH^ψ^†(0))/Z=Tr(ρ^eβH^ψ^(τ−βℏ)e−βH^ψ^†(0))

Now eβH^ψ^(τ−βℏ)e−βH^=eβH^eH^(τ−βℏ)ψ^e−H^(τ−βℏ)e−βH^=eH^τψ^e−H^τ=ψ^(τ).

Therefore:

G(τ−βℏ)=+⟨ψ^(τ)ψ^†(0)⟩=−G(τ)G(τ+βℏ)=−G(τ)

This is the Kubo-Martin-Schwinger (KMS) boundary condition.

Fourier Series and Matsubara Frequencies ​

Due to antiperiodicity on [0,βℏ], we expand G(τ) in a Fourier series with odd harmonics:

G(τ)=1βℏ∑n=−∞∞e−iωnτG(iωn)

where ωn=(2n+1)πβℏ are Matsubara frequencies for fermions.

Why these frequencies? The condition e−iωn(τ+βℏ)=−e−iωnτ requires e−iωnβℏ=−1, so ωnβℏ=(2n+1)π.

The inverse transform:

G(iωn)=∫0βℏdτeiωnτG(τ)

Free Fermions ​

For non-interacting fermions with H^0=∑kεkc^k†c^k:

G0(k,τ)=−⟨Tτc^k(τ)c^k†(0)⟩

For τ>0: G0=−⟨c^k(τ)c^k†(0)⟩=−e−εkτ⟨c^kc^k†⟩=−e−εkτ(1−fk)

where fk=⟨c^k†c^k⟩=1eβεk+1 is the Fermi function.

For τ<0: G0=+⟨c^k†(0)c^k(τ)⟩=+e−εkτ⟨c^k†c^k⟩=+e−εkτfk

Fourier transforming:

G0(iωn)=∫0βℏdτeiωnτ(−e−εkτ(1−fk))=−1−fkεk−iωn(1−e−(εk−iωn)βℏ)

Using eiωnβℏ=−1 and e−βℏεk(1−fk)=fk:

G0(iωn)=1iωn−εk

Part IV: The Spectral Function, LDOS, and Density of States ​

Retarded and Advanced Green's Functions: Definition ​

In Part I, we defined the time-ordered Green's function. For calculating physical response, we need the retarded and advanced Green's functions defined directly in real frequency.

The retarded Green's function is defined as:

GR(r,r′,ω)=∫−∞∞dteiωtGR(r,t;r′,0)

where the real-time retarded function is:

GR(r,t;r′,t′)=−iθ(t−t′)⟨{ψ^(r,t),ψ^†(r′,t′)}⟩

The advanced Green's function is:

GA(r,t;r′,t′)=+iθ(t′−t)⟨{ψ^(r,t),ψ^†(r′,t′)}⟩

For a single-particle system with eigenstates H^|n⟩=En|n⟩, we can compute GR explicitly. Inserting complete sets of eigenstates:

GR(r,r′,ω)=∑n,mϕn(r)ϕm∗(r′)∫−∞∞dteiωt(−i)θ(t)⟨n|{|n⟩⟨n|,|m⟩⟨m|}|m⟩e−i(En−Em)t/ℏ

For fermions, {c^n,c^m†}=δnm, giving:

GR(r,r′,ω)=∑nϕn(r)ϕn∗(r′)ω−En/ℏ+iη

where η→0+ ensures the retarded nature (poles in lower half-plane).

The Spectral Function ​

From the above, the spectral function is defined as:

A(r,r′,ω)=−2ImGR(r,r′,ω)

Computing the imaginary part using Im1x+iη=−πδ(x):

A(r,r′,ω)=2π∑nϕn(r)ϕn∗(r′)δ(ω−En/ℏ)

For the diagonal case r=r′, this relates to the local density of states.

Local Density of States (LDOS) ​

The local density of states at position r and frequency ω is:

ρ(r,ω)=∑n|ϕn(r)|2δ(ω−En/ℏ)

Comparing with the spectral function:

ρ(r,ω)=−1πImGR(r,r,ω)=12πA(r,r,ω)

Physical interpretation: ρ(r,ω)dω is the probability density of finding a state with energy in [ℏω,ℏ(ω+dω)] at position r.

Free Particle: Deriving the Spectral Function ​

For free particles, we found G0(k,ω)=1ω−εk/ℏ+iη. The spectral function is:

A0(k,ω)=−2Im1ω−εk/ℏ+iη=2πδ(ω−εk/ℏ)

This shows the free particle has well-defined energy εk (infinite lifetime, delta function peak).

Density of States in d Dimensions ​

For homogeneous systems, the density of states per unit volume is:

ν(ω)=∫ddk(2π)dδ(ω−εk/ℏ)

Derivation: Integrate the LDOS over all space. Since |ϕk(r)|2=1/V for plane waves:

ν(ω)=1V∑kδ(ω−εk/ℏ)=∫ddk(2π)dδ(ω−εk/ℏ)

For free electrons with εk=ℏ2k22m, change variables to energy. The surface area of a d-dimensional sphere is Sd−1=2πd/2Γ(d/2).

In spherical coordinates:

ν(ω)=Sd−1(2π)d∫0∞dkkd−1δ(ω−ℏk22m)

Let ξ=ℏk22m, then k=2mξ/ℏ and dk=mℏkdξ:

ν(ω)=Sd−1(2π)d(2mωℏ)(d−2)/2mℏθ(ω)

Simplifying using Γ functions:

ν(ω)=1(2π)d/2(mℏ)d/2ωd/2−1Γ(d/2)θ(ω)

Special cases:

  • d=1: ν(ω)∝ω−1/2 (van Hove singularity at band bottom)
  • d=2: ν(ω)=constant (independent of energy)
  • d=3: ν(ω)∝ω1/2 (parabolic increase)

Spectral Representation and Sum Rules ​

The spectral function satisfies important sum rules. From the definition:

∫−∞∞dω2πA(k,ω)=∫−∞∞dω∑n|⟨n|k⟩|2δ(ω−En/ℏ)=∑n|⟨n|k⟩|2=1

This normalization sum rule reflects the completeness of eigenstates.

The first moment gives the average energy:

∫−∞∞dω2πωA(k,ω)=εk

Matsubara to Real Axis: Analytic Continuation ​

For the Matsubara Green's function, the spectral representation is derived by inserting exact eigenstates (Lehmann representation):

G(k,iωn)=∫−∞∞dω′2πA(k,ω′)iωn−ω′

Comparing with the retarded function form, we obtain the analytic continuation prescription:

GR(k,ω)=G(k,iωn→ω+iη)

This prescription allows us to compute equilibrium quantities at Matsubara frequencies, then obtain real-time response by analytically continuing to just above the real axis.

Part V: Interacting Systems and the Dyson Equation ​

Many-Particle Systems ​

Real materials have interactions: electrons repel via Coulomb force. The Hamiltonian:

H^=∑σ∫d3rψ^σ†(r)(−ℏ2∇22m)ψ^σ(r)+12∑σ,σ′∫d3rd3r′V(r−r′)ψ^σ†(r)ψ^σ′†(r′)ψ^σ′(r′)ψ^σ(r)

Self-Energy ​

The full interacting Green's function G can be related to the free G0 via:

G=G0+G0ΣG

or equivalently:

G−1=G0−1−Σ

where Σ is the self-energy. In momentum-frequency space:

G(k,ω)=1ω−εk−Σ(k,ω)

Physical meaning of self-energy:

  • ReΣ: energy shift (effective mass renormalization)
  • ImΣ: damping (inverse lifetime ℏ/τk=−2ImΣ)

For a quasiparticle peak at ω=Ek with small imaginary part:

GR≈Zkω−Ek+i/2τk

where Zk is the spectral weight (quasiparticle residue).

Part VI: Non-Equilibrium Quantum Transport ​

The Problem ​

In quantum transport, we have two reservoirs at different chemical potentials μL≠μR connected by a conductor. There is no global thermal equilibrium.

The Keldysh Contour ​

Extend time from −∞ to +∞ then back to −∞. The contour-ordered Green's function is:

GC(τ,τ′)=−i⟨TCψ^(τ)ψ^†(τ′)⟩

Decomposing into real-time components on the forward (+) and backward (−) branches gives four Green's functions. The useful combinations are:

  • Lesser: G<(t,t′)=+i⟨ψ^†(t′)ψ^(t)⟩ (particle occupation)
  • Greater: G>(t,t′)=−i⟨ψ^(t)ψ^†(t′)⟩ (hole occupation)
  • Retarded: GR=θ(t−t′)[G>−G<]
  • Advanced: GA=θ(t′−t)[G<−G>]

Physical Observables ​

The density and current are directly obtained from G<:

n(r,t)=−iG<(r,t;r,t)J(r,t)=ℏ2m(∇′−∇)r′→r(−i)G<(r,t;r′,t)

The Keldysh Dyson Equation ​

In matrix form:

(GRG<0GA)=(G0RG0<0G0A)+(G0RG0<0G0A)(ΣRΣ<0ΣA)(GRG<0GA)

The equation for G< decouples:

G<=(1+GRΣR)G0<(1+ΣAGA)+GRΣ<GA

For non-interacting systems with no Σ<, and using the boundary conditions from the reservoirs, this yields the Landauer formula.

Summary ​

LevelQuantityKey Result
Single-particle QMG(r,t;r′,t′)Propagator for Schrödinger equation
Statistical mechanicsρ^, G(τ)Thermal averages, Matsubara formalism
Many-body physicsΣ, Dyson equationQuasiparticles with renormalized mass and lifetime
Non-equilibriumG<, GRDensity and transport from contour-ordered Green's functions

References ​

  1. Mahan, G.D. Many-Particle Physics, 3rd ed. (Springer, 2000)
  2. Bruus, H. & Flensberg, K. Many-Body Quantum Theory in Condensed Matter Physics (Oxford, 2004)
  3. Haug, H. & Jauho, A.-P. Quantum Kinetics in Transport and Optics of Semiconductors (Springer, 2008)
  4. Datta, S. Electronic Transport in Mesoscopic Systems (Cambridge, 1995)
